projects.title: Clpr
projects.summary: Full-stack Twitch clip curation platform with community voting, hybrid search, and a React Native mobile app, live in production at clpr.tv.
projects.description: Clpr started as a way to surface the best Twitch clips without relying on platform algorithms. The result is a production content platform with Reddit-style karma voting, hybrid search combining BM25 full-text with semantic vector matching, user collections, and social features. A Go API serves both the React web client and a React Native mobile app built on Expo through a shared JSON contract. Infrastructure is containerized with Docker, orchestrated via Kubernetes, monitored through Prometheus and Grafana, and deployed through GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines.
highlights: Shipped a production web and mobile client with community voting, collections, and hybrid BM25 + semantic vector search.
Built a Go API layer handling auth, content moderation, and real-time feed composition across PostgreSQL, Redis, and OpenSearch.
Deployed to a VPS with Kubernetes orchestration, Prometheus/Grafana monitoring, and automated CI/CD through GitHub Actions.
architecture: Go API with Gin serving both the React web client and the React Native Expo mobile app through a shared JSON contract.
Hybrid search pipeline combining OpenSearch BM25 full-text scoring with semantic vector embeddings for relevance ranking.
PostgreSQL 17 as the primary store, Redis 8 for caching and session management, OpenSearch 2.11 for search indexing; all containerized and orchestrated with Kubernetes.
lessons: Hybrid search is worth the infrastructure cost; users consistently found clips that pure text search missed, and the BM25 + vector blend outperformed either approach alone.
Running a mobile and web client against the same API forced cleaner contract discipline early, which paid off when adding features later.

media.alt: Architecture diagram showing the Clpr platform stack: Go API, React web, React Native mobile, PostgreSQL, Redis, and OpenSearch.
media.caption: Full platform architecture spanning web, mobile, and search infrastructure.

projects.title: Subcorp
projects.summary: Self-hosted multi-agent AI platform where six autonomous agents propose, debate, and execute missions through real-time WebSocket chat with sandboxed tool execution.
projects.description: Subcorp is a self-hosted platform for autonomous AI coordination. Six named agents with distinct personalities and access controls propose missions, debate approaches in roundtable conversations, and execute tasks using a shared toolbox, with minimal human intervention. The system runs on Next.js 16 with React 19, backed by PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector across 32 tables. Agents communicate through real-time WebSocket connections in the Sanctum chat interface, which supports @mention routing, whisper channels, and structured roundtable modes. Each agent has access to sandboxed tools including web search, bash execution, file I/O, and semantic memory retrieval, all running in an isolated Docker container.
highlights: Deployed a live multi-agent system where six AI agents autonomously propose, debate, and execute missions with minimal human intervention.
Built a real-time Sanctum chat interface with WebSocket-driven @mention routing, whisper channels, and structured roundtable conversation modes.
Designed a sandboxed toolbox container giving agents access to bash, Python, Node.js, web search, file I/O, and semantic memory, isolated from the host system.
architecture: Next.js 16 with React 19 server and client components, PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector for semantic memory across 32 tables, OpenRouter SDK for LLM orchestration.
WebSocket layer handles real-time agent-to-agent and agent-to-user communication with structured message routing, @mention parsing, and whisper privacy.
Sandboxed Docker container provides agents with bash, Python, and Node.js execution, web search, and file I/O, network-isolated from the host with resource limits.
lessons: Agent personality and access control boundaries matter as much as the underlying model; the same LLM produces radically different outputs when given distinct identities, relationship dynamics, and tool permissions.
Real-time multi-agent chat requires careful message ordering and conflict resolution; race conditions between agents proposing simultaneous actions were the hardest bugs to debug.

media.alt: Diagram of the Subcorp agent system: six named agents communicating through the Sanctum WebSocket interface with a shared sandboxed toolbox.
media.caption: Multi-agent coordination architecture with real-time WebSocket chat and sandboxed execution.

projects.title: Transcript Create
projects.summary: GPU-accelerated transcription SaaS with Whisper AI, speaker diarization, full-text search, and published client SDKs for Python and TypeScript.
projects.description: Searching inside video content is still broken. Most platforms don't transcribe, and those that do make the output unsearchable. Transcript Create fixes that with GPU-accelerated Whisper transcription, optional pyannote speaker diarization, and full-text search across channels via OpenSearch. A FastAPI backend coordinates GPU workers that scale horizontally using a Postgres SKIP LOCKED queue pattern. Transcripts are stored in PostgreSQL with export support for SRT, VTT, and PDF. The system includes OAuth login, Stripe billing with free and pro tiers, a Vite + React frontend, Prometheus and Grafana monitoring, published client SDKs for Python and TypeScript, and Docker images published to GHCR.
highlights: Built a GPU-accelerated Whisper transcription pipeline with optional pyannote speaker diarization, scaling horizontally via Postgres SKIP LOCKED worker queues.
Published client SDKs for Python and TypeScript, giving third-party developers a typed interface to the transcription and search API.
Shipped full SaaS infrastructure including OAuth login, Stripe billing with tiered plans, and 255 Playwright E2E tests across the stack.
architecture: FastAPI backend orchestrating GPU Whisper workers (ROCm/CUDA) through a Postgres SKIP LOCKED queue; workers scale independently and pick up jobs without coordination overhead.
PostgreSQL as the primary store with OpenSearch indexing for full-text transcript search across channels, with SRT/VTT/PDF export pipelines.
Stripe billing integration with free and pro tiers, OAuth login, Prometheus/Grafana monitoring, and Docker images published to GHCR for reproducible deployments.
lessons: The SKIP LOCKED queue pattern in Postgres eliminated the need for a separate message broker: one fewer service to operate, and the transactional guarantees simplified error recovery.
Publishing typed SDKs early forced the API contract to stabilize faster than internal-only consumption would have; external consumers are unforgiving about breaking changes.

media.alt: Pipeline diagram showing video ingestion, GPU Whisper transcription, speaker diarization, and full-text search indexing.
media.caption: Transcription pipeline from video ingestion through GPU processing to searchable, exportable output.

projects.title: Clustr
projects.summary: Reddit community analysis platform with graph algorithms, Louvain community detection, and interactive 3D/2D network visualization.
projects.description: Clustr crawls Reddit to map relationships between communities, then visualizes the results as interactive network graphs. A Go backend handles API serving, subreddit crawling, and graph precalculation. Data is stored in PostgreSQL and queried through sqlc-generated type-safe code. The frontend renders 3D and 2D force-directed graphs using WebGL, with dashboard and community detection views powered by the Louvain algorithm. The project includes Prometheus and Grafana monitoring, k6 load testing, security audits, and performance profiling.
highlights: Implemented Louvain community detection to identify clusters of related subreddits from crawled relationship data, surfacing non-obvious connections between communities.
Built interactive 3D and 2D force-directed graph visualizations using WebGL, with dashboard views and drill-down into detected community clusters.
Applied production engineering discipline: k6 load testing, security auditing, performance profiling, and Prometheus/Grafana monitoring across the full stack.
architecture: Go backend split into three concerns: API serving, subreddit crawling, and graph precalculation; each independently deployable and monitored.
PostgreSQL with sqlc for type-safe query generation, supporting the graph data model for subreddit nodes, relationship edges, and precomputed community assignments.
Vite + React frontend rendering WebGL force-directed graphs with multiple visualization modes (3D, 2D, dashboard, community detection) and real-time graph interaction.
lessons: Precalculating graph relationships was essential; real-time Louvain detection on the full dataset was too slow for interactive use, but batch precalculation made the frontend feel instant.
Load testing with k6 early in development caught a connection pool exhaustion issue that would have been painful to debug in production with real crawl traffic.

media.alt: 3D network graph visualization showing interconnected Reddit communities with color-coded Louvain clusters.
media.caption: Interactive 3D force-directed graph with Louvain community detection overlays.

projects.title: Internet-ID
projects.summary: Content provenance system anchoring creator ownership on-chain via Solidity smart contracts, IPFS storage, and a browser extension for one-click verification.
projects.description: Content authenticity is collapsing in the AI era. Internet-ID gives creators a pipeline to prove ownership: hash your work, sign a provenance manifest, store it on IPFS, and register the claim on-chain through a Solidity smart contract on L2. The system includes a Next.js App Router frontend, an Express API with Prisma ORM, BullMQ async processing with Redis caching, and a browser extension for Chrome, Firefox, and Safari that enables one-click verification on YouTube and Twitter. Smart contracts are audited with Slither, the frontend meets WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards, and the full stack is tested through backend, web, and E2E CI pipelines.
highlights: Designed a content provenance pipeline: hash content, sign a manifest, pin to IPFS, and register the claim on-chain through a Solidity smart contract on L2.
Built a cross-browser extension (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) for one-click ownership verification directly on YouTube and Twitter pages.
Passed Slither security audits on the ContentRegistry smart contract and met WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards on the frontend.
architecture: Solidity ContentRegistry contract on L2 with Hardhat + TypeScript toolchain, Ethers v6 integration, and Slither static analysis for security auditing.
Next.js App Router frontend with NextAuth, Express API with Prisma ORM, BullMQ async queue for IPFS pinning, and Redis caching for verification lookups.
Browser extension supporting Chrome, Firefox, and Safari, injecting verification UI into YouTube and Twitter pages with cross-origin message passing to the API.
lessons: IPFS pinning reliability varies significantly across providers; abstracting behind Infura, Web3.Storage, and Pinata with automatic failover was necessary for production trust.
Browser extension development across three engines (Chromium, Gecko, WebKit) required more platform-specific code than expected; a shared core with thin platform adapters was the right pattern.

media.alt: Flow diagram showing content hashing, IPFS storage, on-chain registration, and browser extension verification.
media.caption: Content provenance pipeline from creator signing through on-chain anchoring to browser-based verification.

projects.title: SoundHash
projects.summary: Audio fingerprinting system using spectral analysis to match clips across social media, with a FastAPI backend and automated bot integration.
projects.description: SoundHash identifies audio content by processing video and audio through spectral analysis to create compact fingerprint vectors, then matches them against a database of known recordings. The system uses STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) with peak detection to generate fingerprints that are robust to compression, re-encoding, and partial clips. A FastAPI REST API handles ingestion and matching, PostgreSQL stores fingerprints and metadata, and automated bots for Twitter and Reddit can detect and flag matched content. The stack includes JWT authentication, Swagger/ReDoc documentation, an enterprise security layer with WAF and DDoS protection, and an email notification system for match alerts.
highlights: Built an audio fingerprinting pipeline using STFT spectral analysis and peak detection, producing compact vectors robust to compression, re-encoding, and partial clips.
Integrated automated Twitter and Reddit bots that detect and flag matched audio content across social media platforms.
Implemented enterprise security features including WAF, DDoS protection, threat detection, and automated email notifications for match alerts.
architecture: STFT-based spectral analysis extracts frequency peaks, which are hashed into compact fingerprint vectors and stored in PostgreSQL for efficient similarity matching.
FastAPI REST API with JWT auth, Swagger/ReDoc documentation, and a processing pipeline that handles audio extraction from video, fingerprint generation, and database matching.
Social media bot layer integrating Twitter and Reddit APIs for automated content monitoring, with configurable matching thresholds and alert routing.
lessons: Fingerprint robustness matters more than precision: a fingerprint that survives re-encoding and compression catches more real-world matches than one optimized for exact-match accuracy.
Rate limiting on social media APIs shaped the bot architecture more than the audio processing did; batching and backoff strategies were critical for sustained operation.

media.alt: Pipeline diagram showing audio extraction, STFT spectral analysis, fingerprint generation, and cross-platform matching.
media.caption: Audio fingerprinting pipeline from ingestion through spectral analysis to social media matching.

projects.title: Jury-Rigged
projects.summary: Real-time AI courtroom simulation with multi-agent roles, Ace Attorney-style PixiJS rendering, deterministic replay, and Twitch audience interaction.
projects.description: Jury-Rigged is a real-time courtroom simulation where AI agents play judge, prosecutor, defense attorney, witnesses, and bailiff. Cases progress through deterministic phases with live SSE streaming, and a jury system handles voting and deliberation. The visual layer renders proceedings in an Ace Attorney-style format using PixiJS, while an operator dashboard built with React and Vite controls case flow. Every session is recorded as NDJSON for deterministic replay, and Twitch integration lets audiences interact through chat commands like !press and !objection. The system runs on Node.js with TypeScript and Express, using OpenRouter for LLM orchestration.
highlights: Built a multi-agent courtroom simulation with deterministic phase progression, live SSE streaming, and jury voting and deliberation mechanics.
Rendered proceedings in Ace Attorney visual style using PixiJS, with character animations, dialogue boxes, and courtroom transitions.
Integrated Twitch chat commands (!press, !objection) for real-time audience participation, plus NDJSON recording for deterministic session replay.
architecture: Node.js + TypeScript + Express backend orchestrating multiple AI agents through OpenRouter, with deterministic phase state machines governing case progression.
PixiJS rendering layer producing Ace Attorney-style courtroom visuals, character sprites, dialogue overlays, and animated transitions driven by the agent output stream.
Dual interface: a viewer-facing stream with PixiJS rendering and Twitch chat integration, plus an operator dashboard (React + Vite) for case management and phase control.
lessons: Deterministic replay from NDJSON recordings was invaluable for debugging agent behavior; being able to replay exact sequences without LLM variance made the multi-agent interactions far easier to tune.
Twitch chat integration drove more engagement than expected; audience commands like !objection created a feedback loop that made the AI responses feel more dynamic and responsive.

media.alt: Screenshot of the Ace Attorney-style courtroom rendering with AI agent dialogue and Twitch chat overlay.
media.caption: Ace Attorney-style PixiJS rendering with real-time AI agent dialogue and audience interaction.

projects.title: Patchwork
projects.summary: AT Protocol-native mutual aid platform with map-based discovery, community moderation, geoprivacy controls, and Bluesky Jetstream ingestion.
projects.description: Patchwork is a mutual aid platform built natively on the AT Protocol. The system ingests data from Bluesky's Jetstream feed, indexes community posts, and surfaces them through a map-based discovery interface with geoprivacy controls that protect exact user locations. A TypeScript monorepo houses the React web client, a query API, an ingestion and indexing pipeline, and a moderation worker. The architecture prioritizes community safety: moderation runs as a dedicated service, geoprivacy fuzzes coordinates before storage, and the AT Protocol integration means identity and social graph are decentralized rather than platform-owned.
highlights: Built an AT Protocol-native platform ingesting Bluesky Jetstream data for decentralized identity and social graph integration.
Implemented geoprivacy controls that fuzz coordinates before storage, protecting user locations while preserving map-based community discovery.
Shipped a moderation worker as a dedicated service with automated content review, keeping community safety concerns separated from the main application logic.
architecture: TypeScript monorepo (npm workspaces) with four services: React web client, query API, Jetstream ingestion pipeline, and moderation worker.
AT Protocol integration via Bluesky Jetstream for real-time data ingestion, with PostgreSQL storing indexed posts and community metadata.
Geoprivacy layer fuzzes coordinates before persistence, moderation worker processes content asynchronously, and the web client renders map-based discovery through a privacy-aware tile layer.
lessons: Building on the AT Protocol forced good architectural decisions; decentralized identity eliminated the need for a custom auth system, and the social graph came for free.
Geoprivacy is harder than it looks. Simple coordinate rounding creates grid artifacts that can be reverse-engineered; adding controlled random noise within a privacy radius was more resilient.

media.alt: Map-based interface showing community aid posts with geoprivacy-fuzzed location markers and AT Protocol identity integration.
media.caption: Map-based mutual aid discovery with geoprivacy controls and decentralized identity.

projects.title: Galdr
projects.summary: B2B SaaS customer health scoring platform that combines Stripe, HubSpot, and Intercom signals through a Go API and React frontend.
projects.description: Galdr is a customer health scoring platform for B2B SaaS companies. It centralizes customer signals from Stripe, HubSpot, and Intercom, then surfaces automated health scoring through a Go backend and a React 19 + TypeScript frontend. The project ships with PostgreSQL-backed persistence, migration workflows, Dockerized development and production topology, SEO-aware frontend build steps, and a manual GitHub Actions deployment flow that targets a VPS behind Nginx.
highlights: Built Galdr as a customer health scoring product for B2B SaaS teams that combines Stripe, HubSpot, and Intercom data into an automated scoring workflow.
Shipped a full-stack implementation with a Go net/http API, React 19 + TypeScript frontend, PostgreSQL 16 persistence, and Docker-based dev/prod workflows.

Added production-facing delivery concerns including manual GitHub Actions VPS deployment, Nginx-backed container orchestration, frontend SEO artifact generation, and a dedicated Stripe billing domain.
architecture: Go backend organized around internal handler, middleware, model, repository, and service layers, with migrations managing the PostgreSQL 16 data model.
React 19 + TypeScript frontend built with Vite and TailwindCSS v4, including SEO validation, sitemap artifact generation, and prerender steps in the frontend build pipeline.
Containerized development and production topology using Docker Compose, Nginx, and a manual GitHub Actions deploy workflow that targets a VPS and verifies both database readiness and API health.
lessons: Separating billing into its own domain makes a SaaS product easier to evolve; the dedicated Stripe billing endpoints and environment contract keep payment concerns from bleeding into the core scoring flows.
Treating SEO and deploy automation as first-class build concerns pays off early; validating SEO artifacts and codifying the VPS deployment path reduces launch friction later.

projects.title: Cutroom
projects.summary: Collaborative AI video production pipeline where specialized agents research, script, voice, edit, and publish short-form videos with structured handoffs and attribution tracking.
projects.description: Cutroom is a collaborative short-form video production system built for agent-native creative workflows. The product orchestrates a seven-stage pipeline from research through publishing, with each stage claimable by a specialized agent and every handoff represented as structured output. The stack pairs a Next.js 14 + React 18 dashboard with API routes, Prisma-backed PostgreSQL persistence, Remotion video rendering, and integrations for ElevenLabs voice generation, Pexels-powered visuals, and tokenized attribution on Base. The hosted app at cutroom.subcult.tv exposes templates, pipeline views, and examples for the end-to-end creative flow.
highlights: Built a seven-stage agent-native production pipeline that turns a topic into a published short-form video through structured handoffs.
Shipped a template-driven creative system with real-time pipeline tracking, attribution weights, and token-based reward logic for contributors.
Integrated Remotion rendering, ElevenLabs voice synthesis, and multi-platform publishing workflows behind a single hosted dashboard.
architecture: Next.js 14 app-router frontend and API surface share a TypeScript codebase, with dashboard pages, REST endpoints, and pipeline orchestration living in the same application boundary.
Prisma-backed PostgreSQL persistence stores pipelines, stages, and attribution metadata while Remotion assembles the final video output from structured stage artifacts.
External integrations connect voice generation, visual sourcing, and Base token distribution so the production flow can move from topic intake to published asset without ad hoc handoff glue.
lessons: Structured agent handoffs matter more than raw model quality in creative pipelines; the clearer the schema between stages, the less rework the editor stage has to absorb.
Treating attribution as a first-class system concern changes the product architecture early, because contribution tracking has to be threaded through every stage rather than bolted on at the end.

projects.title: Subcults
projects.summary: Privacy-first platform for underground music communities with map-based discovery, scene identity, live audio sessions, and AT Protocol ingestion.
projects.description: Subcults is a platform for underground and local music communities built around presence, scene sovereignty, and privacy-first discovery. It maps scenes, events, and live audio sessions without leaning on opaque follower-feed ranking, instead combining trust-aware discovery, decentralized AT Protocol ingestion, and coarse-location controls. The stack uses a Go API and Jetstream indexer, a Vite + React + TypeScript frontend with MapLibre, Neon Postgres with PostGIS, LiveKit for audio sessions, Cloudflare R2 for media, and Stripe Connect for direct scene payouts. The hosted site at subcults.subcult.tv is the public-facing home for that product direction.
highlights: Designed a scene-first platform for underground music communities with map-based discovery, scene identity, and privacy-aware event publishing.
Combined AT Protocol Jetstream ingestion, live audio sessions, and direct revenue tooling so scenes can coordinate culture without ceding control to algorithmic feeds.
Built privacy and operational rigor into the platform early with coarse-location controls, EXIF stripping, observability, security scanning, and legal/privacy documentation.
architecture: Go API and Jetstream indexer handle core application flows plus decentralized AT Protocol ingestion, with Neon Postgres 16 + PostGIS storing spatial and trust-aware community data.
Vite + React + TypeScript frontend uses MapLibre for scene and event discovery, while LiveKit powers real-time audio rooms and Cloudflare R2 stores media assets and recordings.
Production-oriented Docker Compose deployment runs API, indexer, and frontend behind an external reverse proxy, with metrics, vulnerability scanning, and performance budgets built into the workflow.
lessons: Privacy constraints reshape product design in a good way; when precise location is optional and consent-based, map and feed workflows have to become clearer and more intentional.
Underground-community tooling needs governance and observability as early as discovery features, because trust and safety are part of the product, not an afterthought.
